concepts.title: Aufbau principle
concepts.conceptBody:
Aufbau Principle

An atom in its lowest state of energy is said to be in ground state. The ground state is the most stable state in an atom. According to Aufbau principle:

"electrons are added progressively to the various orbitals in their order of increasing energy starting with the orbital of lowest energy."

The order of increasing energy may be summed up as follows:

$1~\text{s},2~\text{s},2\text{p},3~\text{s},3\text{p},4~\text{s},3~\text{d},4\text{p},5~\text{s},4~\text{d},5\text{p},6~\text{s},4\text{f},5~\text{d},6\text{p},7~\text{s},5\text{f},6~\text{d},\ldots .$.

As a working rule, a new electron enters an empty orbital for which the value of $(n+l)$ is minimum. If the value of $(n+l)$ is same for two or more orbitals, the new electron enters an orbital having lower value of $n$.

concepts.title: Pauli's exclusion principle
concepts.conceptBody:
Pauli Exclusion Principle

According to this principle: 

"no two electrons in an atom can have the same set of all the quantum numbers or one can say that no two electrons can have the same quantised states."

Consider an electronic arrangement in Ist energy level $(\text{n}=1)$. For $\text{n}=1,\text{l}=0,\,\,\And \,~\text{m}=0$. Now $\text{s}$ can have two values corresponding to each value of $m$ i.e. $s=+1/2,-1/2$. Hence, the possible designation of an electron in a state with $\text{n}=1$ is $1,0,0,+1/2$ and $1,0,0,-1/2(\text{n},1,~\text{m},~\text{s})$ i.e., two quantised states. This implies that an orbital can accommodate (for $\text{n}=1,~\text{m}=0,\Rightarrow $ one orbital) maximum of two electrons having opposite spins.

The maximum number of electrons in the different subshells are:

$\text{ s sub-shell }=2,\text{p sub-shell }=6,~\text{d sub-shell }=10\text{ and f sub-shell }=14.\text{ }$

concepts.title: Hunds rule of maximum multiplicity
concepts.conceptBody:
Hund's Rule of Maximum Multiplicity

According to this rule: 

"electrons never pair until no available empty degenerate orbitals are left to them."

This means an electron always occupies a vacant orbital in the same sub-shell (degenerate orbital) and pairing starts only when all of the degenerate orbitals are filled up. This means that the pairing starts with 2nd electron in s sub-shell, 4th electron in p sub-shell, 6th electron in d sub-shell and 8th electron in $\text{f}$ sub-shell.

By doing this, the electrons stay as far away from each other as possible. This is highly reasonable if we consider the electron-electron repulsion. Hence electrons obey Hund's rule as it results in lower energy state and hence more stability.

concepts.title: Extra stability of half filled and completely filled orbitals
concepts.conceptBody:
Extra Stability of Half and Fully Filled Orbitals

A particularly stable system is obtained when a set of equivalent orbitals (degenerate orbitals) is either fully filled or half filled, i.e., each containing one or a pair of electrons. This effect is more dominant in $\text{d}$ and $\text{f}$ sub-shells.

This means three or six electrons in $p$ sub-shell, five or ten electrons in $d$ sub-shell, and seven or fourteen electrons in $\text{f}$ sub-shell forms a stable arrangement. Note this effect when filling of electrons takes place in d sub-shells (for atomic numbers $\text{Z}=24,25$ and 29,30 ).

concepts.title: Ground state electronic configuration of elements
concepts.conceptBody:
Ground state electronic configuration of elements

In the following table you should analyse how to employ the above rules to write electronic configuration of various elements.

Electronic configuration of an element is represented by the notation $n{{l}^{x}}$:

$\text{x}$: number of electrons present in an orbital

1: denotes the sub-shell

$\text{n}$: principal quantum number

Ground-State Electronic Configuration of the Elements

Note: In atomic numbers, 24 and 29, observe the rule of extra-stability of half filled and fully filled orbitals.

Example

(a) Find the orbital angular momentum of an electron in the following orbital

(i) $3p$ (ii) 3d (iii) $3~\text{s}$

(b) Arrange the electrons represented by the following sets of quantum numbers in decreasing order of energy

(i) $\text{n}=4,\text{l}=0,~\text{m}=0,~\text{s}=\pm 1/2$ (ii) $\text{n}=3,\text{l}=2,~\text{m}=0,~\text{s}=\pm 1/2$

(iii) $\text{n}=3,\text{l}=1,~\text{m}=1,~\text{s}=-1/2$ (iv) $\text{n}=3,\text{l}=0,~\text{m}=0,~\text{s}=-1/2$

Solution. 

(a) $\mu =\sqrt{l(l+1)}\frac{\text{h}}{2\pi }=\sqrt{l(l+1)h}$

(i) For 3p, $l=1,{{\mu }_{1}}=\sqrt{1(1+1)}h=\sqrt{2}h$

(ii) For 3d, $l=2,{{\mu }_{1}}=\sqrt{2(2+1)}\frac{h}{2\pi }=\sqrt{6}h$

(iii) For 3s, $l=0,{{\mu }_{1}}=0$

(b) Higher the value of (n + l) higher will be the energy of orbital. If the two values are same then one having higher value of $n$ the energy will be higher

For (i) $(n+l)=44$ s orbital

For (ii), $(\text{n}+\text{l})=5,3~\text{d}$ orbital

For (iii) $(\text{n}+\text{l})=43\text{p}$ orbital

For (iv), $(\text{n}+\text{l})=33~\text{s}$ orbital

Decreasing order of energy: (ii) >$(i)$>$(iii)$> (iv)
